coredata.dc:title: Pectin/carbon quantum dots fluorescent film with ultraviolet blocking property through light conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using glucose as a carbon source through a hydrothermal method. CQDs showed negligible cytotoxicity to L929 cells even after prolonged exposure of 72 h. The addition of CQD did not affect the pectin film's mechanical properties, water contact angle, and thermal stability. However, the CQD-added composite film generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), providing high antibacterial activity against pathogenic bacteria (L. monocytogenes and E. coli) and antifungal activity against mold (Aspergillus flavus), where a 100% eradication of bacteria and fungi population was observed. Also, the addition of CQD strengthens the antioxidant activity of the composite films by 95%. Further, the CQD-added pectin film converted ultraviolet rays into blue light, which improved the film's UV protection properties. Therefore, the pectin/CQD film has a high potential for a light conversion active packaging film that may prevent the deterioration of high-fat foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Antimicrobial activity
Antioxidant activity
Ultraviolet blocking
Light conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tensile strength (TS) and water contact angle (WCA)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF ) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. 2022R1A2B02001422 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Surface and cross-sectional SEM images and (b) AFM patterns of the pectin-based films.
(a) FTIR and (b) XRD spectra of the pectin-based films. (c) EDX and (d) elemental mapping of pectin/CQD2 film at a 1 µm scale showing the presence and distribution of carbon (blue) and oxygen (red).
TGA and DTG thermograms of the pectin-based films.
Tensile strength and water contact angle of the pectin-based films. Different letters on each bar indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).
The CQD release rate (a-c) and antioxidant activity (d) of the pectin-based films.

Antimicrobial activity of the pectin-based films determined by (a) agar well diffusion method and (b-d) colony counting method against Listeria monocytogenes, Escherichia coli, and Aspergillus flavus, and (e) ROS generation capacity of the pectin-based films.
(a) Light absorbance spectra, (b) fluorescence spectra, and visual appearance of the pectin-based films under (c) visible and (d) UV light.
Schematic illustration for fabrication of CQD incorporated pectin-based film.
Apparent color and light transmittance of the pectin-based films.

head.ce:title: Pectin/carbon quantum dots fluorescent film with ultraviolet blocking property through light conversion

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding, Acquisition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BioNanocomposite Research Center, Department of Food and Nutrition, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using glucose as a carbon source through a hydrothermal method. CQDs showed negligible cytotoxicity to L929 cells even after prolonged exposure of 72 h. The addition of CQD did not affect the pectin film's mechanical properties, water contact angle, and thermal stability. However, the CQD-added composite film generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), providing high antibacterial activity against pathogenic bacteria (L. monocytogenes and E. coli) and antifungal activity against mold (Aspergillus flavus), where a 100% eradication of bacteria and fungi population was observed. Also, the addition of CQD strengthens the antioxidant activity of the composite films by 95%. Further, the CQD-added pectin film converted ultraviolet rays into blue light, which improved the film's UV protection properties. Therefore, the pectin/CQD film has a high potential for a light conversion active packaging film that may prevent the deterioration of high-fat foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Most foods lose value due to deterioration or spoilage during storage or distribution after processing. A strategy traditionally used to address this problem is to add additives to the food itself, but this is losing popularity as consumers become increasingly aware of the harmful effects of chemical preservatives in food [1]. An alternative approach that has become increasingly popular in recent years is active packaging, which involves the deliberate addition of active substances, such as antimicrobials and antioxidants, to the packaging materials that interact either with the packed food or with the product headspace, thereby complementing the basic functionality of the packaging material [2,3]. Nanomaterials such as nanoclays, nanometals, and nano metal oxides are the most commercially available active additives. Biopolymer-based nanocomposite films have been developed using a variety of nanofillers because they enhance the films' physical and functional characteristics [4,5]. According to a recent study, a coating composed of carrageenan and TiO2 nanotubes could improve applications for photocatalytic activity. Another study utilized carboxymethyl cellulose-based films with zinc oxide nanoparticles for their antibacterial properties [6]. Zinc oxide nanoparticles were used in the multifunctional films to increase the shelf life of packaged beef by preventing microbial growth. However, most of them are manufactured by chemical routes, so they are expensive and relatively unsafe for direct or indirect human contact applications [7]. In addition, much research is being done on sustainable, safe, and economical alternative additives such as plant extracts and essential oils [8,9]. However, these natural active substances could not be standardized because their composition differed depending on the raw material, resulting in differences in function [10]. Therefore, recent research on active packaging has focused on sustainable, natural, and safe bio-derived functional additives [11,12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as an attractive option in active packaging applications due to their unique optical functional properties [13–15]. Technically, the term 'quantum' refers to a discrete unit of physical property. CQDs are zero-dimensional nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in size, providing a three-dimensional limited electron motion for quantum mechanics to determine the properties of these nanoparticles [16–18]. CQDs manufactured by a facile sustainable synthetic process using various bio-based sources have been utilized in biomedical applications, including drug delivery, bioimaging, bio-labeling, and photodynamic therapy, confirming their safety in human contact applications [1]. They also show high adsorption interactions with biological surfaces thanks to their small size with controllable surface functional groups [16,19]. In addition to their biological properties, some recent studies have reported CQDs' strong antibacterial and potent antioxidant activity [20,21]. Unlike other high molecular weight metal and semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs show other beneficial properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, etc. [22,23]. CQDs have also gained popularity due to their unique photoluminescent properties that differentiate them from other carbon-based nanomaterials through their source-dependent absorption properties, whose characteristic light absorption is usually in the UV region. CQD displays an excellent UV-blocking effect and is a good candidate for an anti-UV material, inhibiting UV-mediated oxidation of food [24]. These properties make them attractive materials to explore in packaging applications [25,26].

Biopolymers, such as polysaccharides, proteins, lipids, and their blends, are attracting attention as suitable substrates for using CQDs in food packaging because of their biodegradability, sustainability, biocompatibility, and excellent film-forming properties [27]. Pectin is a widely used biopolymer for manufacturing packaging films due to its flexibility, stabilization, texture, and good gel-forming ability [28]. Pectin, present in the primary cell walls and intercellular regions of higher plants, is a water-soluble acidic heteropolysaccharide with varied structures but dominantly possessing a linear chain of α-(1−4)-linked D-galacturonic acid units with varying proportions of methoxyl or methyl ester, and has been used to develop biopolymer-based composite films [29]. Various polymers such as pectin/gelatin, gelatin/carrageenan, regenerated cellulose, poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), zein, and bacterial nanocellulose have been tested to prepare composite films as matrices for CQD addition [1]. Hess et al. prepared a PVA/polyethyleneimine/citric acid-based film containing CQDs. This film had greater than 90% protection against UV-A radiation, comparable to UV-protected polyethylene terephthalate films [30]. In a recent study, we prepared composite films using pectin and gelatin using various ratios of CQDs, finding that composite films containing sulfur-functionalized CQDs exhibited excellent UV protection properties [14]. CQD-added light-converting packaging films are one of the intriguing properties that offer new directions for applications of these quantum dots, but the little investigation has been studied yet.

Compared to other natural sources, glucose is equipped with the advantages of providing stable and strong luminescent CQD, raising it a perfect candidate for synthesizing purposes. Glucose is composed of carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen and is considered a pure CQD source due to the elimination of oxygen and hydrogen and the remaining pure carbon through synthesis [13,22]. Therefore, in this study, CQD was prepared using glucose, a safe organic source containing many hydroxyl groups, and attempted to manufacture a light conversion film by integrating them with a pectin-based film. To this end, the physicochemical and functional properties of the CQD-added pectin film were evaluated, and the efficacy of the film against ultraviolet light was tested.

Materials and methods for the synthesis and characterization of CQD, fabrication of pectin-based composite films incorporated with CQD, characterization and determination of their properties were described in Supporting Information. A schematic diagram of the methodology for synthesizing CQD and fabricating composite films is presented in Scheme 1.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the pectin-based films are shown in Fig. 1a. The uniform distribution of CQDs in the pectin matrix is presumably due to the hydrophilic property of CQDs. Previously, CQD has been used in packaging films, and biocompatibility depends on the CQD concentration and interaction with the polymer matrix [15]. However, it can be seen that CQD does not form aggregates even at relatively high concentrations and is uniformly dispersed in the biopolymer film matrix, indicating excellent biocompatibility with biopolymers.

The surface roughness of the pectin-based films depended on the CQD concentration (Fig. 1b). The film's average roughness (Ra) increased slightly when 1–3 wt% of CQDs were added. The roughness of the CQD-added pectin film indicates that the addition of CQDs showed consistent interactions with the film matrix without major changes throughout the film to form compatible and smooth films, consistent with the SEM results.

The changes in the molecular structure of the composite films by the addition of CQDs were evaluated using FTIR analysis, and the resulting FTIR spectra of the pectin-based films are shown in Fig. 2a. Two characteristic bands observed at 3308 and 2930 cm−1 in all films were due to the hydroxyl (O-H) stretching and methyl and methylene (C-H) stretching vibrations of symmetric and asymmetric groups in pectin biopolymer. Absorption bands found at 1643 and 1437 cm−1 were attributed to the carboxyl groups (-COOH) stretching of symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration. The absorption bands at 1739 and 1228 cm−1 were due to the carbonyl groups (CO) and carbon-oxygen single bond (C-O). No noticeable change in band position was found. Overall, the FTIR study showed that the addition of nanofillers produced similar spectra without altering the structure of the biopolymer, indicating that the addition of CQD did not change the chemical structure of the polymer [31–33]. However, the intensities of some peaks varied due to van der Waals interactions between polymers and nanoparticles [34]. A similar phenomenon has been observed in the cellulose nanofiber-based films incorporated with CQDs, prepared using glucose and nitrogen functionalized CQDs [13]. They stated that the -COOH stretching vibrations shifted from 1602 cm−1 to 1604 cm−1. The FTIR spectra indicated that CQDs formed hydrogen bonds with the -COOH groups of pectin without chemical interaction.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was used to test the effect of CQDs on the crystal phase of the polymer matrix. The XRD patterns of pectin film showed weak wide broadband peaks around 2Ɵ = 13 and 20.0º and a weak refraction peak at 2Ɵ = 28.0º, indicating the amorphous properties of pectin [35]. When CQD was added, there was a change in the refractive peaks of the pectin films (Fig. 2b). The peak intensity at 2Ɵ = 28.0º was weakened by adding 1 and 2 wt% of CQD, and it disappeared with the addition of 3 wt% of CQD. Even if CQDs interfere with the intermolecular hydrogen bonding of pectin biopolymers, their presence does not fundamentally change the crystal structure of the underlying matrix [36]. These results are evidence of the coexistence of the two phases of CQD and pectin polymer [37].

Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) and elemental mapping of CQD-added film were performed to determine the presence of the elemental compounds. Fig. 2c-d shows the EDX spectra and elemental mapping of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) of the pectin/CQD2 film, which shows that the CQD was evenly dispersed between the C and O elements.

The release of chemical compounds is of paramount importance, especially when considering films in food packaging applications during the thermal process, such as microwave heating. The thermal stability of CQD-added films was evaluated using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA and DTG), and the results are shown in Fig. 3. The pectin-based films showed two-step thermal degradation patterns, consistent with the previously reported pectin-based films [29]. The first stage of degradation was observed at 80–120 ℃ due to moisture evaporation. The second degradation occurred at 170–240 ℃ with a maximum of around 210–230 ℃, attributed to the evaporation of glycerol and pectin degradation. Despite the hydrophilicity of CQDs, the TGA and DTG patterns of the pectin film and the CQD-added composite film were almost identical. Overall, adding CQDs did not affect the thermal stability of the pectin-based films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile strength (TS) and water contact angle (WCA)
ce:section.ce:para: Packaging films must meet adequate mechanical strength and integrity, mainly related to the concentration of the filler, the interaction between the film matrix and the filler, and the internal structure of the film matrix itself. The behavior of the film as a function of the force applied to it was analyzed as tensile strength (TS), defined as the maximum strength that the active film can withstand. The TS of a composite film is highly dependent on the compatibility of the filler with the polymer matrix and the filler's distribution state. The mechanical strength of the ideal active film must be sufficient to maintain its integrity and prevent packaging failure during production, storage, and distribution [38]. Packaging films with a TS value of 65–75 MPa are generally considered to have good mechanical quality. As shown in Fig. 4, the TS of the pectin-based films was about 40 MPa, indicating that the neat pectin film is relatively weaker than other polymer films.

The strength of the pectin film increased when 1 wt% of CQD was added and showed no significant change when the CQD content was high (2–3 wt%). The difference in film TS is due to the distribution of CQDs. CQDs at the low concentration were evenly distributed in the polymer matrix and acted as a crosslinking agent to increase the pectin film's mechanical strength (TS) [39]. In contrast, CQDs at high concentrations may form aggregates in the film, the ordered molecular arrangement of the film matrix is destroyed, and the film strength is decreased because the fillers are not evenly distributed in the polymer matrix [40]. These results suggested that the outcomes of physicochemical properties, including mechanical properties, SEM, AFM, and EDX, were consistent, showing that the presence of CQDs formed hydrogen bonds with pectin without chemical interaction and did not change the structure of the underlying matrix.

The surface hydrophilicity of the pectin-based films was evaluated by measuring water contact angle (WCA), and the results are shown in Fig. 4. The WCA of the neat pectin film was 46.7°, indicating that the film had a hydrophilic surface. The surface hydrophilicity of the pectin films slightly increased (i.e., decrease in WCA) when 1 and 2 wt% CQDs were added; however, the hydrophilicity of the film was not affected when 3 wt% CQDs were added. The reduced WCA (i.e., increased hydrophilicity) of the CQD-added films was mainly due to the hydrophilicity of the CQDs. On the other hand, it has also been reported that self-aggregation of CQD (in the case of high concentration) can increase the WCA of the CQD-added films [1].

The release of additives from packaging films is an important issue in food packaging applications. Several studies of the release behavior of nanoparticles have shown that the release properties are often affected by the swelling index of the polymer matrix and other factors such as water solubility, nanoparticle dispersion, and solubility in aqueous media [41]. The release of CQDs from the pectin-based films was measured in water and various concentrations of ethanol (10%, 50%, and 95%) to determine the CQD release behavior, and the results are shown in Fig. 5a-c. CQDs were released rapidly during the first hour and reached a steady state after 2 h. In the case of pectin/CQD1 and pectin/CQD2 films, the maximum release rate of CQD was obtained in 10% alcohol, followed by water, 50% ethanol, and 95% ethanol. In the case of pectin/CQD3 film, CQDs were released faster in water than in alcohol solutions, which may be attributed to the extra surface hydroxyl groups of CQD, thereby enhancing their interaction with an aqueous solution (water). Overall, the release behavior of CQDs depended on the alcohol concentration, which directly affected the polymer matrix [24]. Aqueous solutions (water and 10% ethanol) are sufficiently hydrated to penetrate the hydrophilic pectin matrix and form more open structures, resulting in faster CQDs migrating from the polymer to the aqueous solvent.

On the other hand, high concentrations (50% and 95%) of ethanol restricted access to pectin due to its low solubility and swelling rate in alcohol, forming a tight structure and trapping the CQDs inside the matrix. As a result, the release rate of CQD depends on the type of solvent, the swelling property of the polymer, and the affinity of CQD to the environmental solvent. Therefore, it is expected that the high release of CQDs could confer more functional properties when the film is in direct contact with food.

Ensuring food safety and maintaining quality is a prerequisite for food packaging research to extend the shelf life of food by developing multifunctional packaging films with antioxidant, antibacterial, and UV protection functions. One of the main challenges facing the food industry is to prevent the deterioration of food quality due to fat oxidation, especially in high-fat foods. Research on preventing food oxidation using antioxidant packaging films is attracting great attention. The antioxidant activity of pectin-based films was tested against long-acting free radicals using the ABTS [2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)] and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl radical) methods, and the results are shown in Fig. 5d. The neat pectin film showed a slight antioxidant activity of 7.2% and 4.2% of ABTS and DPPH radical scavenging activity.

On the other hand, the CQD-added films showed significantly higher antioxidant activity of almost 95% and 92% regardless of the CQD concentration (1, 2, or 3 wt%). The superior antioxidant properties of the composite films are due to CQDs ability to reduce and annihilate free radicals ABTS• and DPPH• by forming reactive oxygen species (ROS). In other words, electron transfer, hydrogen donation, and adduct formation from hydroxyl groups on the surface form the more stable products ABTS-H and DPPH-H [42]. Similar results were reported when incorporating CQDs into cellulose nanofiber films, and 98.6% and 79.4% scavenging powers were obtained for the ABTS and DPPH methods [13]. In addition, the film exhibited a solvent-dependent radical scavenging behavior. However, the difference between ABTS and DPPH scavenging ability is related to the base solvent used for both methods. The aqueous solution used in the ABTS method causes the polymer to swell more. The hydrophilic CQDs can diffuse and release more in the solution, consistent with the release test results showing the higher release of the CQDs in an aqueous solution. Nevertheless, it is expected that CQD-added films exhibit excellent antioxidant activity in both methods, allowing easy application of the films in packaging systems to prevent oxidative degradation of foods.

The antimicrobial properties of packaging materials are paramount when considering their potential applications in active food packaging to ensure food safety and extend shelf life. The antimicrobial property of CQD-added films was evaluated qualitatively by the agar well diffusion method against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and A. flavus. In this test, the inhibition zone's size indicates the microorganisms' susceptibility to the films. It is worth mentioning that the films' hydrophilicity, dispersion, and diffusion capacity have a critical impact on facilitating the larger diameter of the inhibition zone. The neat pectin film exhibited no antimicrobial activity, while CQD-added films showed strong antibacterial and antifungal properties. As shown in Fig. 6a, the inhibition zone of microbial growth was proportional to the concentration of added CQD. The pectin/CQD1 film showed inhibition zones of 3 ± 1, 1 ± 1, and 4 ± 2 mm against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and A. flavus, which increased to 7 ± 1, 5 ± 1, and 10 ± 2 mm when exposed to the pectin/CQD2 film. The pectin/CQD3 film exhibited higher antimicrobial activity with 7 ± 1, 8 ± 1, and 12 ± 2 mm inhibition zone.

In addition, the inhibitory effect of the films on microbial growth was quantitatively evaluated by the total viable colony count method against L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and A. flavus. The neat pectin film showed similar bacterial and fungal growth as the control (Fig. 6b-d), indicating that the neat pectin film had no antimicrobial activity.

On the other hand, the CQD-added films showed significant antimicrobial activity against test bacteria and fungi, and the antimicrobial activity increased according to the concentration of CQDs. At a low concentration of CQD (1 wt%), the film retarded the growth of L. monocytogenes and showed almost 4 Log CFU/mL lower growth than the control at 6 h incubation and completely stopped the growth of L. monocytogenes after 9 h. While, a bacteriostatic effect was observed for E. coli and A. flavus, and the number was about 6 Log CFU/mL lower than that of the control group at the end of 12 h. The pectin/CQD2 film completely inhibited the growth of L. monocytogenes and A. flavus after 3 h and 2 days of incubation, respectively, and showed slightly higher antibacterial activity against E. coli. The pectin/CQD3 film showed a clear bactericidal and fungicidal effect and completely inhibited their growth after incubation for 3 h and 1 day of bacteria and fungi, respectively.

The antimicrobial activity of the CQD-added film is due to the excellent antimicrobial activity of CQD. Numerous studies have elucidated the efficacy of CQDs and their mechanism of action on microorganisms. The antimicrobial properties of CQDs depend on several factors such as specific surface functional groups, electrostatic interactions, small size, and reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. One of the accepted antimicrobial mechanisms of CQDs is the formation of ROS, including hydroxyl free radicals, singlet oxygen, and superoxide anions [43]. Therefore, the ROS-forming ability of the CQD-added films was evaluated using the ORAC (oxygen radical absorbance capability) method (Fig. 6e). The neat pectin film did not form ROS within 1 h, whereas the CQD-added films gradually increased ROS generation according to the amount of CQDs, forming effective free radicals. The ROS-forming ability, according to the concentration of CQD-added films, coincided with the antimicrobial activity of the film, which means that the antimicrobial activity of the pectin/CQD films depends on the ROS-generating ability of CQD. Furthermore, CQDs can exert antibacterial action through electrostatic interaction with microorganisms by van der Waals attraction, as Travlou et al. reported. In addition, penetration of CQDs into the cell wall results in disruption of metabolic processes, protein breakdown and disruption of DNA replication, failure of cellular machinery, and subsequent cell lysis [43].

The color and optical properties of packaging films influence the consumer's first impression and are the basic functions of protecting packaged food from photochemical reactions. The color of biopolymer-based packaging films is often influenced by the addition of active substances used to impart functional properties. Considering the brown color of CQD, the color of the CQD-added composite film can cause a visually significant color change. Generally, stimulus color values, L-value (lightness), a-value (greenness/redness), and b-value (blueness/yellowness), are used to measure the surface color of the packaging films. The pectin-based films' optical properties and visual appearance are shown in Table 1 and Fig. 7. The surface color of the pectin-based films was greatly affected by the concentration of CQD added. As the CQD concentration increased, the brightness (L-value) of the film decreased, and redness (a-value) and yellowness (b-value) increased, making it darker and brownish. As a result, the film's total color difference (ΔE) increased, mainly due to the brown color of the CQDs. Roy et al. (2021) and Ezati, Roy, et al. (2021) also found that biopolymer-based films such as gelatin/carrageenan and pectin/gelatin composite films became darker by adding CQDs [14,40]. The L-values of the films decreased, but the a-value and b-values increased. The addition of CQD gives the film a tan, which is also evident in the apparent photographic image of the film (Fig. 7).

The light-shielding property of the film for food packaging is another functional property that blocks the light of a specific wavelength and prevents photochemical reactions, thereby protecting the quality of foods that are easily deteriorated by photocatalytic oxidation [44,45]. The UV–vis absorption spectra at 200–800 nm and the light transmittance at 280 and 660 nm of the pectin-based films were measured to evaluate the UV–vis blocking properties of packaging films, and the results are also shown in Table 1 and Fig. 7a. The CQD-added composite films prevented more than 90% of UV transmission (T280), whereas transparency (T660) decreased by less than 1% when 3 wt% of CQD was added. Although the light absorption characteristics of CQDs depend on the raw materials, they generally exhibit light absorption characteristics in the UV region (230–320 nm). For the carbon core, the absorption bands are due to the π–π * transition of aromatic CC bonds or the n-π * transition of CO/CN bonds [46]. The absorption properties of CQDs are affected by the types and content of surface groups, size of π-conjugated domains, and variation of the oxygen/nitrogen content in carbon cores. The excited-state intramolecular proton transfer by O-H-O, O-H-N, tunnels and ample conjugated structures are responsible for the barrier property in the CQD-added films [47]. The light-blocking domain of the films expanded as the concentration of CQD in the film increased. These results confirm the feasibility of pectin films with CQDs for use in applications such as food packaging and light conversion acting as UV protection films.

Accordingly, the pectin film exhibits an excitation spectrum peak at 359 nm, which is boosted by the addition of CQD in the pectin/CQD2 film, as seen in Fig. 7b. Additionally, the strongest excitation wavelength is used to measure the emission spectra of films. From the results, pectin film's emission spectrum ranges from 350 to 700 nm, with a peak at 447 nm. However, a significant difference was observed in the shape and intensity of the emission peak shifts for pectin/CQD2 film, which was most likely because of the quantum confinement effect. There was a clear and bathochromic shift of emission peak with higher peak intensity located at 436 nm in pectin/CQD2 film. Variations in emissions are largely due to the passivated surface defects of carbon dots, and the degree of carbonization in the core-shell structure is believed to be a crucial determinant [48]. The composite film exhibits ultraviolet excitation and blue emission, suggesting a potential use as a light conversion film. The light conversion effect of the CQD-added film was evaluated using UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm as a light source. As shown in Fig. 7 c,d, the neat pectin film showed no appreciable blue fluorescence under UV light.

In contrast, the film to which CQD was added showed an intense blue luminescence proportional to the concentration of added CQD. It can be seen that the CQD-added film has ultraviolet excitation and blue emission capabilities. Therefore, the pectin/CQD film can efficiently convert UV light into blue light when considering the potential for application as a light conversion film in food packaging. Consequently, introducing CQDs into pectin films can induce UV blocking properties without sacrificing the transparency of the films, suggesting great potential for use as transparent fluorescent films in active packaging applications.
The data presented as mean ± SD. Any two means with different lowercase letters in the same column are not significantly (p < 0.05) different by Duncan’s multiple range tests.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the most sustainable and environmentally friendly quantum dots because they use carbon sources that are abundantly available in nature for their synthesis. CQDs have been used in fluorescence sensing studies due to their unique non-toxicity, hydrophilicity, small size, and stable optical properties such as outstanding photostability and photoluminescence properties. Glucose was used to synthesize CQDs with stable and strong luminescence using a hydrothermal method. An in vitro cytotoxicity test (shown in Supporting Information) confirmed the safety and biocompatibility of CQD. The viability of L929 mouse fibroblasts exposed to different concentrations of CQD was determined by MTT assays for 72 h, indicating more than 90% cell viability at a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. The CQDs were integrated into pectin film to prepare pectin/CQD films using a solution casting method. The addition of CQD did not affect the film's mechanical strength, water contact angle, and thermal stability while significantly increasing the antioxidant, antibacterial, antifungal, and UV blocking properties of pectin-based films. The UV-barrier and light conversion properties of CQD-added film were also investigated, showing their capability to convert UV light into blue light due to the high photoluminescence property of CQDs. The excellent antibacterial, antifungal, and antioxidant activity of CQD-added pectin films with fluorescent properties make them potential packaging films for sensing or active food packaging applications.

Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding, Acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could affect the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) (No. 2022R1A2B02001422) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2022.112804.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots synthesized from microorganisms and food by-products: active and smart food packaging applications
Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Nutr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Moradi, R. Molaei, S.A. Kousheh, J.T. Guimarães, D.J. McClements, Carbon dots synthesized from microorganisms and food by-products: active and smart food packaging applications, Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Nutr. 0 (2021) 1–17. https://doi.org/10.1080/10408398.2021.2015283.
